questions.question: What is the main idea of bidirectional typing?
options: Split typing into synthesis and checking judgments
questions.explanation: Bidirectional systems use two complementary judgments to control inference complexity and improve clarity.

questions.question: What is the difference between synthesis and checking mode in bidirectional typing?
options: Synthesis infers types bottom-up; checking verifies types top-down against an expected type
Synthesis is for runtime, checking for compile time
Checking produces types, synthesis consumes them
questions.explanation: In synthesis mode the term produces its type; in checking mode the term is verified against a given expected type.

questions.question: Why do lambdas often need expected types in bidirectional checkers?
options: Their parameter type may not be inferable without a checking target
questions.explanation: Checking a lambda against an expected arrow type provides parameter/domain information needed for body checking.

questions.question: What is a practical benefit of bidirectional typing over unrestricted inference?
options: More predictable algorithms and clearer annotation boundaries
questions.explanation: Bidirectional structure makes inference behavior more tractable and diagnostics easier to localize.

questions.question: How does bidirectional typing relate to type annotation burden?
options: It reduces annotations to strategic insertion points where inference is ambiguous
It has no effect on annotations
questions.explanation: Bidirectional systems typically need annotations only at 'turning points' — places where the mode switches from checking to synthesis.

_meta.title: Phase Capstone - A Bidirectional Type-Checker for STLC + Extensions
